did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a self-supervised learning model (Histomorphological Phenotype Learning with the BarlowTwins algorithm) to analyze skin biopsy whole slide images (H&E stained) for the diagnosis of Mycosis Fungoides, a cutaneous T-cell lymphoma. The AI model achieved high accuracy (96-98%) in classifying MF vs non-MF cases.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is an abstract from the 67th ASH Annual Meeting presenting original experimental results. It describes a study design involving a self-supervised learning model (HPL with BarlowTwins) trained on 300 sections from 50 MF patients and 300 from 50 non-MF patients, with internal and external validation. The abstract reports specific quantitative results (accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, F1, AUC) derived from this new data analysis, fitting the criteria for a research paper (specifically a conference abstract with original results).

title: Diagnosing mycosis fungoides using AI powered histologic analysis
brief_summary: This study aimed to enhance the diagnosis of Mycosis Fungoides (MF), a cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, using AI-powered histologic analysis to overcome diagnostic challenges posed by benign inflammatory conditions. The researchers employed a self-supervised learning model, Histomorphological Phenotype Learning with the BarlowTwins algorithm, to analyze hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stained whole slide images. The model was trained on 300 sections from 50 MF patients and 300 from 50 non-MF patients sourced from NYU Langone Health, with external validation performed on an independent dataset from the University of Minnesota. In the internal NYU test set, the model achieved 96% accuracy, 98% sensitivity, and an AUC of 96%, while the external validation yielded 98% accuracy and 100% sensitivity without additional fine-tuning. Attention heatmaps revealed distinct diagnostic feature patterns predominantly in the epidermis, dermis, or at the epidermal-dermal junction. The study concludes that AI-trained models can accurately diagnose MF solely based on H&E images, potentially improving diagnostic speed and reducing reliance on ancillary testing.

lesion_types_studied.details: Early-stage Mycosis Fungoides, a cutaneous T-cell lymphoma. Used as the positive class in the binary classification task (MF vs non-MF). Training dataset included 300 sections from 50 patients; external validation included 50 cases.

Benign inflammatory conditions used as the negative class to distinguish from MF. Specifically included spongiotic and eczematous dermatitis. Training dataset included 300 sections from 50 patients; external validation included 50 cases. The abstract also mentions dermatitis and psoriasis as conditions with shared histological features, but the experimental dataset explicitly lists spongiotic and eczematous dermatitis.

all_task_types.target: Slide-level diagnosis of Mycosis Fungoides (MF) vs non-MF inflammatory skin conditions
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task predicting discrete diagnostic categories (MF vs non-MF) using whole slide images. The model achieved 96% accuracy, 98% sensitivity, and an AUC of 96% on the internal NYU test set, and 98% accuracy, 100% sensitivity, and an AUC of 95.5% on the external University of Minnesota validation set.

datasets_used.name: NYU Langone Health Dataset
datasets_used.description: Internal dataset comprising skin biopsy whole slide images (H&E stained, scanned at 20x magnification) from NYU Langone Health collected between 2021 and 2024.
classes_used: Non-MF inflammatory skin conditions (spongiotic and eczematous dermatitis)

use.training: Used for training the self-supervised learning model (HPL with BarlowTwins) on 70% of the data.
use.validation: Used for validation on 10% of the data.
use.testing: Used for internal testing on 20% of the data.

datasets_used.name: University of Minnesota Dataset
datasets_used.description: External independent validation dataset comprising skin biopsy cases from the University of Minnesota.

use.testing: Used for external validation without additional fine-tuning.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported descriptive performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1 score, AUC) for the AI model on internal and external validation sets.

NYU Langone Health (Internal Test Set).notes: The model identified seven enriched and four depleted image tile clusters in MF vs non-MF cases. Attention heatmaps showed distinct patterns of diagnostic patches, with subsets of cases showing predictive clusters predominantly in the dermis, epidermis, or epidermal-dermal junction.

University of Minnesota (External Valida.notes: Performance was nearly identical to the internal test set. Attention heatmaps highlighted three patterns of diagnostic feature enrichment: predominantly epidermal, dermal, or at the epidermal-dermal junction.

architecture_used.main_model: Histomorphological Phenotype Learning (HPL) with the BarlowTwins algorithm

architecture_details.input_processing: Whole slide images (WSI) scanned at 20x magnification, tiled into 224px sections

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Histomorphological Phenotype Learning (HPL) with the BarlowTwins algorithm
preprocessing_techniques: Whole slide images (WSI) scanned at 20x magnification
conclusions: Self-supervised learning models can accurately diagnose Mycosis Fungoides (MF) solely based on H&E stained skin biopsy images.

The AI model achieved high diagnostic performance with 96% accuracy and 98% sensitivity on the internal NYU test set, and 98% accuracy and 100% sensitivity on the external University of Minnesota validation set without additional fine-tuning.

Attention heatmaps revealed distinct patterns of diagnostic feature enrichment, with predictive clusters located predominantly in the epidermis, dermis, or at the epidermal-dermal junction, reflecting biological diversity in neoplastic cell infiltration.
AI-trained models have the potential to increase the speed and accuracy of MF diagnosis.
AI models may minimize the use of resource-intensive ancillary testing in MF, potentially leading to improved patient outcomes.
important_remarks: The study is a conference abstract (ASH 2025) rather than a full peer-reviewed journal article, which may limit the depth of methodological detail and statistical reporting available for critical appraisal.

The dataset is small (100 total patients: 50 MF, 50 non-MF) and derived from only two institutions (NYU Langone Health and University of Minnesota), raising concerns about generalizability and potential selection bias.

The non-MF comparator class was limited to spongiotic and eczematous dermatitis; while the background mentions psoriasis and dermatitis as differential diagnoses, the model was not explicitly validated against these other common inflammatory conditions in the reported results.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported; performance metrics are presented as point estimates without measures of variance or significance.

The model was applied to the external validation set without additional fine-tuning, which demonstrates robustness but also highlights the risk of performance degradation if the external data distribution differs significantly from the training set.

Data leakage risk exists if patient-level splitting was not strictly enforced, although the abstract states sections were split by percentage; with only 50 patients per class, ensuring no overlap of patient data between training and test sets is critical but not explicitly detailed in the abstract.
The study focuses on early-stage MF; the model's performance in advanced stages or different clinical presentations is unknown.

The reliance on H&E staining alone means the AI model does not replace the need for clinical correlation or ancillary testing (IHC/molecular) in complex cases, despite the claim that it could minimize their use.
